fib-optimization-ageout-time.description:
Configures FIB (Forwarding Information Base) Optimization route age-out time.If a host route entry is
designated for ageing, and no traffic is using the route for configured amount of age time, the route
entry will be removed from the ASIC. When traffic using that route resumes,
the route entry will be inserted back to ASIC.

fib-optimization-exclude-nexthop-ipv4.description:
Configures next-hops to be excluded from FIB (Forwarding Information Base) optimization.
User may want to prevent optimization of host routes which resolves to certain next hops.
This leaf-list configures such next hops.Any EVPN host route pointing to the configured next hops,
will not be optimized by the AIF feature.

items.description:

User may want to prevent optimization of host routes which resolves to certain next hops.
This leaf-list configures such next hops.Any EVPN host route pointing to the configured next hops,
will not be optimized by the AIF feature.

ArubaAspathList_AspathListsSchemaGET.description: AS Path Lists configuration. The AS Path is the sequence of Autonomous System numbers traversed by a BGP route announcement. This container manages AS Path Lists for filtering and controlling BGP routes based on path characteristics using permit/deny actions and regular expression patterns

as-path-list-entry.description: AS Path List entries with permit or deny actions. Each entry specifies a regular expression pattern to match Autonomous System paths in BGP route advertisements. Entries are evaluated sequentially in ascending order by sequence number, allowing first-match policy enforcement for path-based route filtering and manipulation

aspaths.description: Regular expression pattern to match AS paths. Patterns can include AS numbers and regular expression metacharacters for flexible AS path matching. Example: ^65000

seq-no-optype.description: Key combination of seq-no and optype separated by ~

aspath-list-name.description: Unique name identifier for the AS Path List. This name is referenced in route-maps and BGP routing policies to apply AS Path-based filtering. The name is used as a configuration key to identify specific AS Path filtering rules across the routing policy domain

description.description: Optional description for the AS Path List. Provides documentation about the purpose, use case, and context for this AS Path List in BGP routing policies for operational clarity and maintenance

as-path-list-entry.description: AS Path List entries with permit or deny actions. Each entry specifies a regular expression pattern to match Autonomous System paths in BGP route advertisements. Entries are evaluated sequentially in ascending order by sequence number, allowing first-match policy enforcement for path-based route filtering and manipulation

aspaths.description: Regular expression pattern to match AS paths. Patterns can include AS numbers and regular expression metacharacters for flexible AS path matching. Example: ^65000

seq-no-optype.description: Key combination of seq-no and optype separated by ~

aspath-list-name.description: Unique name identifier for the AS Path List. This name is referenced in route-maps and BGP routing policies to apply AS Path-based filtering. The name is used as a configuration key to identify specific AS Path filtering rules across the routing policy domain

as-path-list.description: AS Path List definition with a unique name identifier. Each list contains a sequence of entries with permit or deny actions and regular expression patterns to match Autonomous System paths in BGP route advertisements. AS Path Lists are used in route-maps to control route acceptance, rejection, or modification based on the path characteristics, enabling sophisticated BGP routing policies and AS-based traffic engineering

ArubaBfd_BfdSchemaGET.description:
BFD global configuration.
Bidirectional Forwarding Detection (BFD) is a protocol intended to detect
faults in the bidirectional path between two forwarding engines, including
interfaces, data link(s), and to the extent possible the forwarding engines
themselves, with potentially very low latency.  It operates independently of
media, data protocols, and routing protocols.

disable-echo.description: Enable or disable BFD echo packets on the system..

echo-source-address.description: Configure the source IP address for BFD echo packets

oneOf.x-typeDescription:
An IPv4 address in dotted quad notation using the default
zone.

An IPv6 address represented as either a full address; shortened
or mixed-shortened formats, using the default zone.

use-alias.description: Echo source address use alias or not

min-echo-rx-interval.description:
Sets or resets the minimum BFD echo packet reception interval.
The value 0 means that the host is not interested in receiving BFD echo packets.
The value itself is a request to the remote system that it might not be able to
honor (ex. we ask for echo packets faster than it can send them). In this scenario,
the remote system is expected to use its fastest/slowest setting (depending on
what we asked) and the session might flap.

This config is available globally and per-interface.

This config is available globally and per-interface.

ArubaBgp_BgpGlobalBase.description: Global configuration parameters for the BGP router

confederation.description: BGP Confederation configuration. A BGP Confederation enables dividing a large Autonomous System into smaller sub-AS groups while maintaining the logical appearance of a single AS to external peers. This feature improves scalability by confining iBGP relationships within sub-AS groups while presenting as a single AS externally

default-bgp-attributes.description: Default BGP administrative distance configuration. Administrative Distance (AD) determines route preference when multiple routing protocols offer routes to the same destination. This configuration sets default distance values for routes learned from external BGP (eBGP), internal BGP (iBGP), and locally configured sources. Lower distances indicate higher preference. Typical defaults are: eBGP 20, iBGP 200, local 200

default-originate.description: Originate default route to BGP neighbors

graceful-restart.description: BGP Graceful Restart (GR) mechanism configuration. GR enables high availability during planned and unplanned BGP process restarts by preserving BGP routes and maintaining data plane forwarding while control plane sessions are reestablished. Supporting routers recognize the restart and gracefully handle route withdrawal and re-advertisement, minimizing traffic disruption

max-prefix-options.description:
Parameters relating to the limit for maximum prefix
configuration options.

route-selection-options.description: Route Selection Options for BGP best path computation. Configures BGP best path selection algorithms and tie-breaker rules including AS path length comparison, Multi-Exit Discriminator (MED) handling, route reflector cluster ID behavior, and path origin validation. These options directly influence how BGP selects the preferred path among multiple routes to the same destination, affecting traffic engineering, load balancing, and failover behavior

timers.description: BGP Timers configuration block for controlling BGP session establishment, maintenance, and failure detection. Includes keep-alive timer (frequency of keepalive messages to maintain active sessions), hold-time (maximum time between consecutive BGP messages before session termination), and connect-retry timer (wait time before attempting reconnection after session failure). These timers are critical for tuning BGP convergence speed and network stability

ArubaBgp_BgpGlobalConfederation.description:
Configuration options specifying parameters when the local
router is within an autonomous system which is part of a BGP
confederation.

identifier.description:
Confederation identifier for the autonomous system.
Setting the identifier indicates that the local-AS is part
of a BGP confederation.

member-as.description:
Remote autonomous systems that are to be treated
as part of the local confederation.

items.description:
Remote autonomous systems that are to be treated
as part of the local confederation.
items.x-typeDescription:
A numeric identifier for an autonomous system (AS). An AS is a
single domain, under common administrative control, which forms
a unit of routing policy. Autonomous systems can be assigned a
2-byte identifier, or a 4-byte identifier which may have public
or private scope. Private ASNs are assigned from dedicated
ranges. Public ASNs are assigned from ranges allocated by IANA
to the regional internet registries (RIRs).

open-on-accept.description:

ArubaBgp_BgpGlobalGracefulRestart.description: Configuration parameters relating to BGP graceful restart.

graceful-restart-enable.description: Enable graceful restart for BGP instance

restart-time.description:
Estimated time (in seconds) for the local BGP speaker to
restart a session. This value is advertise in the graceful
restart BGP capability.  This is a 12-bit value, referred to
as Restart Time in RFC4724.  Per RFC4724, the suggested
default value is <= the hold-time value.

stale-routes-time.description:
An upper-bound on the time thate stale routes will be
retained by a router after a session is restarted. If an
End-of-RIB (EOR) marker is received prior to this timer
expiring stale-routes will be flushed upon its receipt - if
no EOR is received, then when this timer expires stale paths
will be purged. This timer is referred to as the
Selection_Deferral_Timer in RFC4724

ArubaBgp_BgpGlobalRouteSelectionOptions.description:
Set of configuration options that govern best
path selection.

always-compare-med.description:
Compare multi-exit discriminator (MED) value of the routes
from peers of different ASes when selecting the best route.

as-path-multipath-relax.description:
Consider two or more BGP routes as ECMP routes when
routes AS PATH length is same but AS PATHs are different.

compare-med-confederation.description:
Compare multi-exit discriminator (MED) value of the routes
from peers of different ASes within a confederation when
selecting the best route.

compare-originator-id.description:
When comparing similar routes received from external
BGP peers, use the originator-id as a criterion to select
the active path.

compare-router-id.description:
When comparing similar routes received from external
BGP peers, use the router-id as a criterion to select
the active path.

deterministic-med.description:
Compare multi-exit discriminator (MED) value of the routes
advertised by different peers in the same AS when selecting
the best route.

ignore-as-path-length.description:
Ignore the AS path length when selecting the best path.
The default is to use the AS path length and prefer paths
with shorter length.

med-missing-as-worst.description:
Consider MED value as worst (0xFFFFFFFF), if MED
attribute is missing in the routes when selecting
the best route.

ArubaBgp_BgpNeighborGrouping.description:
Configuration parameters relating to a base BGP neighbor that
are also applicable to peer group context.

as-override.description:
Replace all occurrences of the peer AS number with the
router's own AS number before advertising the route.

auth-password.description:
Configures an MD5 authentication password for use with
neighboring devices.
auth-password.x-typeDescription:
A custom derived type for special use cases. This type takes a plaintext password and uses
Vault's 3rd party API to encrypt the password. The intended use case is to encrypt and store
customer credentials which will be used later to login to 3rd party services.

description.description:
An optional textual description (intended primarily for use
with a peer or group

enabled.description:
Whether the BGP peer is enabled. In cases where the
enabled leaf is set to false, the local system should not
initiate connections to the neighbor, and should not
respond to TCP connections attempts from the neighbor. If
the state of the BGP session is ESTABLISHED at the time
that this leaf is set to false, the BGP session should be
ceased.

fall-over.description: Fall-over the BGP session when route to the peer fails.

fall-over-bfd.description:
Enable BFD session with the neighbor and fall-over the BGP
session when connectivity with the neighbor is lost.

ignore-leading-as.description:
Accept the received routes from the peer even if it does
not have their own AS appended to the as-path.

local-as.description:
The local autonomous system number that is to be used
when establishing sessions with the remote peer or peer
group, if this differs from the global BGP router
autonomous system number.

local-as-mode.description: The different modes of using ASN as local AS.

x-enumDescriptions.NO_PREPEND:
The session is established via alternate local-as.
Both the alternate-as and primary-as are not prepended to the inbound AS_PATH list,
but prepended to the outbound AS_PATH list.
x-enumDescriptions.NO_PREPEND_REPLACE_AS:
The session is established via alternate local-as.
Both the alternate-as and primary-as are not prepended to the inbound AS_PATH list,
but alternate local-as is prepended to the outbound AS_PATH list.
x-enumDescriptions.NO_PREPEND_REPLACE_AS_DUAL_AS:
The session can be established using either primary-as or alternate local-as.
Selected-as is not prepended to the inbound AS_PATH list,
but prepended to outbound AS_PATH list.

local-as-mode.x-typeDescription: The modes of using specified ASN as local AS for forming BGP sessions with peers.

remove-private-as.description:
Remove private AS numbers from updates sent to peers - when
this leaf is not specified, the AS_PATH attribute should be
sent to the peer unchanged

use-med.description:
Enable the comparison of MED attribute for the same route
received from two different autonomous systems.

weight.description: This is the weight for routes from this neighbor.

ArubaBgp_BgpSchemaGET.description:
Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) is the standard exterior gateway
protocol (RFC 4271) for exchanging routing information between
autonomous systems on the Internet and enterprise networks. BGP
supports IPv4/IPv6 unicast, EVPN (Ethernet VPN), and multicast
address families with advanced features including route reflection,
confederation, graceful restart, and multi-path load balancing.
Configure BGP global parameters, neighbor relationships, route
redistribution, and address family-specific policies. Requires route
map, prefix list, keychain, and community list configurations for
routing policy enforcement. Use this API to retrieve BGP
configuration.

profile.description:
BGP routing profile containing a single router instance per
Autonomous System. Each entity is identified by profile name.
Configure AS number, VRF instances, neighbors, route policies,
timers, and address families. Use these APIs to create, modify, or
delete a BGP profile.

platform-type.description: Type of the platform (GW, PVOS, CX).
platform-type.x-typeDescription: This value represents the type of Aruba platform.

router.description: BGP Router instance. Each BGP profile supports only ONE router instance (single Autonomous System). The router is identified by a unique AS number that must be consistent across the profile. Multiple AS numbers are not allowed within the same BGP profile. Each instance contains complete BGP configuration including neighbor/peer relationships (internal iBGP and external eBGP), routing policies, keepalive/hold timers, address family configurations (IPv4, IPv6, VPNv4), graceful restart, route reflection, and best path selection

platform-type.description: Type of the platform (GW, PVOS, CX).
platform-type.x-typeDescription: This value represents the type of Aruba platform.

router.description: BGP Router instance. Each BGP profile supports only ONE router instance (single Autonomous System). The router is identified by a unique AS number that must be consistent across the profile. Multiple AS numbers are not allowed within the same BGP profile. Each instance contains complete BGP configuration including neighbor/peer relationships (internal iBGP and external eBGP), routing policies, keepalive/hold timers, address family configurations (IPv4, IPv6, VPNv4), graceful restart, route reflection, and best path selection

ArubaCommunityList_CommunityListsSchemaG.description: BGP Community Lists configuration. A BGP Community is a 32-bit value used to classify and group routes for policy application. This container manages three types of Community Lists: Standard (specific community values), Expanded (regular expression patterns), and Extended (route targets and extended community formats). Each list contains ordered entries with permit or deny actions for route classification and policy enforcement

community-list.description: BGP Community List definition with a unique name and type specification. Each list contains an ordered sequence of entries with permit or deny actions and community values in the format specified by the list type: Standard (specific numeric community values), Expanded (regular expression patterns), or Extended (extended community values and route targets for VPN and EVPN services)

community-list-entry.description: Community list entries with permit or deny actions. Each entry specifies community values or patterns to match in BGP routes. Entries are processed sequentially by sequence number

community-expanded.description: Regular expression pattern to match BGP communities in expanded format. Patterns can include community values and regular expression metacharacters for flexible matching

ArubaEvpn_EvpnArpNdProxyConfig.description: Configuration data related to ARP-Proxy and ND-Proxy

arp-suppression.description:
Enable (TRUE) or disable (FALSE) ARP suppression. If true
the  Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) queries for an
IP address that is not on that network are suppressed.
Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) suppression is a
technique used to reduce the amount of ARP broadcast
flooding within individual VXLAN segments, that is between
VMs connected to the same logical switch.

arp-suppression-extended.description:
Enable (TRUE) or disable (FALSE) ARP suppression. If true
the  Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) queries for an
IP address that is not on that network are suppressed.
Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) suppression is a
technique used to reduce the amount of ARP broadcast
flooding within individual VXLAN segments, that is between
VMs connected to the same logical switch. This configuration
is mutually exclusive with arp-suppression.
Basically arp-suppression-extended provides
complete functionality of arp-suppression on L2-only
VLANs as well as on Active Gateway SVIs.

nd-suppression.description:
Enable (TRUE) or disable (FALSE) Neighbor Discovery suppression.
If true the NDP queries for an IP address that is not on that
network are suppressed. NDP suppression is a
technique used to reduce the amount of NDP Packets
flooding within individual VXLAN segments, that is between
VMs connected to the same logical switch.

nd-suppression-extended.description:
Enable (TRUE) or disable (FALSE) Neighbor Discovery suppression.
If true the NDP queries for an IP address that is not on that
network are suppressed. NDP suppression is a
technique used to reduce the amount of NDP Packets
flooding within individual VXLAN segments, that is between
VMs connected to the same logical switch. This configuration
is mutually exclusive with nd-suppression.
Basically nd-suppression-extended provides
complete functionality of nd-suppression on L2-only
VLANs as well as on Active Gateway SVIs.

mac-mobility-threshold.description:
The number of MAC mobility events that are detected for a
given MAC address within the detection-window before it is identified
as a duplicate MAC address. Once the detection threshold is reached,
updates for the MAC address are suppressed.

mac-mobility-window.description:
The time interval used in detecting a duplicate MAC address.
The value can be from 1 through 1000 seconds.

mac-mobility-threshold.description:
The number of MAC mobility events that are detected for a
given MAC address within the detection-window before it is identified
as a duplicate MAC address. Once the detection threshold is reached,
updates for the MAC address are suppressed.

mac-mobility-window.description:
The time interval used in detecting a duplicate MAC address.
The value can be from 1 through 1000 seconds.

redistribute-local-svi.description:

ArubaEvpn_EvpnSchemaGET.description: Ethernet Virtual Private Network (EVPN) container. EVPN provides Layer 2 and Layer 3 VPN services using BGP control plane and VXLAN/MPLS data plane encapsulation for seamless multi-site connectivity

virtual-links.description:
In Open Shortest Path First (OSPF), a virtual link is a logical path that
connects areas of a network that are not physically connected.
Virtual links are used when it's not possible to connect all areas
of an OSPFv2 autonomous system to the backbone area (area 0).
They can also be used to connect two parts of a partitioned backbone.

Virtual links cannot be configured through backbone area or stub/nssa area.

ArubaOspfv2_Ospfv2SchemaGET.description:
OSPFv2 (Open Shortest Path First version 2) is a routing protocol which is
described in RFC2328 entitled OSPF Version 2. It is a Link State-based
IGP(Interior Gateway Protocol) routing protocol. It is widely used in medium
to large-sized enterprise networks.

The characteristics of OSPFv2 are:

- Provides a loop free topology using SPF algorithm.
- Allows hierarchical routing using area 0 (backbone area) as the top of the
 hierarchy.
- Supports load balancing with equal cost routes for same destination.
- OSPFv2 is a classless protocol and allows for a hierarchical design with
 VLSM(Variable length subnet masking) and route summarization.
- Provides authentication of routing messages.
- Scales enterprise size network easily with area concept.
- Provides fast convergence with triggered, incremental updates via
 Link State Advertisements (LSAs).

profile.description: OSPFv2 configuration profiles for organizing router instances.

description.description: Description of the OSPFv2 profile purpose and configuration.

name.description: Profile name for the OSPFv2 configuration group.

routers.description: The list of all the OSPFv2 Router instances.

areas.description:
The list of all the OSPF area for the OSPF router instance.

An area in OSPF is a logical collection of routers, links and
OSPF networks that have the same area ID. Areas are used to divide
an OSPF network into sub-domains. The main benefit of creating areas
is that it reduces the number of routes that need to be propagated.

backbone-stub-default-route.description: Advertise default route to stub if loopback in backbone area.

default-information-origin.description:
Generate a default external route into an ospfv2 routing domain.
Only one among 'always', 'metric', 'always-metric' can be configured

distance.description:
OSPFv2 distance.

Administrative Distance is used as criteria to select the source of the
route when the same route is learned from different routing protocols.
Optionally, Administrative Distance can be set for each OSPFv3 route type
intra-area, inter-area, and external type-5 and type-7 routes.

distribute-filter.description:
Used to filter routes from being installed in the routing table or
redistributed into another routing protocol.
When inbound direction is used it filter routes to be installed in the routing table.
Outbound direction will filter only the desired redistributed routes from other protocols.
The prefix-list which is provided in command will be used to filter routes for both
inbound and outbound direction. Configuration is not allowed if the referenced
prefix-list has not yet been configured.

prefix.description: Prevent redistribution of routes via OSPF.

oneOf.x-typeDescription:
An IPv4 prefix represented in dotted quad notation followed by
a slash and a CIDR mask (0 <= mask <= 32).

An IPv6 prefix represented in full, shortened, or mixed
shortened format followed by a slash and CIDR mask
(0 <= mask <= 128).

exclude-mask.description: Mask for the network to be excluded for the OSPFv2 instance.
exclude-mask.x-typeDescription:
An IPv4 address in dotted quad notation using the default
zone.

exclude-network.description: Network to be excluded for the OSPFv2 instance.
exclude-network.x-typeDescription:
An IPv4 address in dotted quad notation using the default
zone.

graceful-restart-cfg.description:
Graceful restart OSPF.
If `strict-lsa-check` option is mentioned, the router
will not perform strict LSA check when acting as a restart helper
for a restarting peer. By default, router will participate
in the graceful restart of a neighbor with strict LSA check enabled.

instance-tag-vrf.description: Key combination of instance-tag and vrf separated by ~

max-metric.description:
sets the protocol to advertise a maximum metric so that other
routers do not prefer the router as an intermediate hop in their shortest
path first (SPF) calculations.
- `include-stub` option configures the router to advertise maximum
  metric for links including stub links in router LSA.
- `on-startup` option configures the router to advertise maximum metric in
  router LSA at startup for the time mentioned in seconds or for
  a default value of 600 seconds.
- `include-stub` and `on-startup` options together configure the router
  to advertise maximum metric for links including stub links in router LSA at
  startup for the time mentioned in seconds or for a default value of 600 seconds.

maximum-paths.description: Maximum number of equal cost paths that are stored for each destination in the Routing table.

x-enumDescriptions.TYPE1: Comparable (an OSPFv2 metric plus the external metric)

nonstop.description: Enables or disable nonstop forwarding for OSPFv2.

passive-interface-default.description:
All OSPFv2 enabled interfaces to be passive.
An OSPFv2 passive interface is a network interface
that does not send or receive OSPFv2 Hello packets,
but still participates in the routing process for subnet advertisement.

redistribute-routes.description: Redistribute routes from other sources into this OSPFv2 instance.

reference-bandwidth.description: The reference bandwidth, Mbits/second, for interface cost calculations.

restrict.description: Prevent redistribution of routes via OSPF.

oneOf.x-typeDescription:
An IPv4 prefix represented in dotted quad notation followed by
a slash and a CIDR mask (0 <= mask <= 32).

An IPv6 prefix represented in full, shortened, or mixed
shortened format followed by a slash and CIDR mask
(0 <= mask <= 128).

rfc1583-compatibility.description:
Compatible with RFC 1583.
The difference between RFC 1583 and RFC 2328 is about
how a router chooses the best route among multiple external routes.

router-id.description: The router identifier for the OSPFv2 instance.
router-id.x-typeDescription:
An IPv4 address in dotted quad notation using the default
zone.

subnet-exclude.description: Networks to be excluded for the OSPFv2 instance.

exclude-network-mask.description: Key combination of exclude-network and exclude-mask separated by ~

summary-address.description:
Summarizes the external routes with the matching address or masks.
When advertising this route, its, metric would be set to the lowest
cost path from among the routes that were summarized.
With no-advertise flag enabled, Suppress routes that match the
specified prefix/mask pair.
Only works for ASBR(Autonomous System Boundary Router).

ArubaOspfv3_Ospfv3SchemaGET.description:
OSPFv3 (Open Shortest Path First version 3) is a routing protocol which is
described in RFC5340 entitled OSPF for IPv6. It is a Link State-based IGP
(Interior Gateway Protocol) routing protocol. It is widely used with medium to
large-sized enterprise networks.

The characteristics of OSPFv3 are:

- Provides a loop free topology using SPF algorithm.
- Allows hierarchical routing using area 0 (backbone area) as the top of the
  hierarchy.
- Supports load balancing with equal cost routes for same destination.
- OSPFv3 is a classless protocol and allows for a hierarchical design with
  VLSM (Variable Length Subnet Masking) and route summarization.
- Scales enterprise size network easily with area concept.
- Provides fast convergence with triggered, incremental updates via
  Link State Advertisements (LSAs).

areas.description:
The list of all the OSPFv3 area for the OSPFv3 router instance.

An area in OSPFv3 is a logical collection of routers, links and
OSPFv3 networks that have the same area ID. Areas are used to divide
an OSPFv3 network into sub-domains. The main benefit of creating areas
is that it reduces the number of routes that need to be propagated.

OSPFv3 distance.

Administrative Distance is used as criteria to select the source of the
route when the same route is learned from different routing protocols.
Optionally, Administrative Distance can be set for each OSPFv3 route type
intra-area, inter-area, and external type-5 and type-7 routes.

graceful-restart-cfg.description:
Graceful restart OSPF.
If `strict-lsa-check` option is mentioned, the router
will not perform strict LSA check when acting as a restart helper
for a restarting peer. By default, router will participate
in the graceful restart of a neighbor with strict LSA check enabled.

max-metric.description:
sets the protocol to advertise a maximum metric so that other
routers do not prefer the router as an intermediate hop in their shortest
path first (SPF) calculations.
- `include-stub` option configures the router to advertise maximum
  metric for links including stub links in router LSA.
- `on-startup` option configures the router to advertise maximum metric in
  router LSA at startup for the time mentioned in seconds or for
  a default value of 600 seconds.
- `include-stub` and `on-startup` options together configure the router
  to advertise maximum metric for links including stub links in router LSA at
  startup for the time mentioned in seconds or for a default value of 600 seconds.

redistributed into another routing protocol.

The prefix-list which is provided in command will be used to filter routes for both
inbound and outbound direction. Configuration is not allowed if the referenced
prefix-list has not yet been configured.

When inbound direction is used it filter routes to be installed in the routing table.
Outbound direction will filter only the desired redistributed routes from other protocols.
The prefix-list which is provided in command will be used to filter routes for both
inbound and outbound direction. Configuration is not allowed if the referenced
prefix-list has not yet been configured.

router-id.description: The router identifier for the OSPFv3 instance.
router-id.x-typeDescription:
An IPv4 address in dotted quad notation using the default
zone.

summary-aggregate-route.description:
Summarizes the external routes with the matching address or masks.
When advertising this route, its, metric would be set to the lowest
cost path from among the routes that were summarized.
With no-advertise flag enabled, Suppress routes that match the
specified prefix/mask pair.
Only works for ASBR(Autonomous System Boundary Router).

description.description: Description of the OSPFv3 profile purpose and configuration.

name.description: Profile name for the OSPFv3 configuration group.

routers.description: The list of all the OSPFv3 Router instances.

active-backbone-stub.description: Advertise default route to stub if loopback in backbone area.

areas.description:
The list of all the OSPFv3 area for the OSPFv3 router instance.

An area in OSPFv3 is a logical collection of routers, links and
OSPFv3 networks that have the same area ID. Areas are used to divide
an OSPFv3 network into sub-domains. The main benefit of creating areas
is that it reduces the number of routes that need to be propagated.

default-info-originate.description:
Generate a default external route into an ospfv2 routing domain.
Only one among 'always', 'metric', 'always-metric' can be configured

distance.description:
OSPFv3 distance.

Administrative Distance is used as criteria to select the source of the
route when the same route is learned from different routing protocols.
Optionally, Administrative Distance can be set for each OSPFv3 route type
intra-area, inter-area, and external type-5 and type-7 routes.

prefix.description: Prevent redistribution of routes via OSPF.
prefix.x-typeDescription:
An IPv6 prefix represented in full, shortened, or mixed
shortened format followed by a slash and CIDR mask
(0 <= mask <= 128).

graceful-restart-cfg.description:
Graceful restart OSPF.
If `strict-lsa-check` option is mentioned, the router
will not perform strict LSA check when acting as a restart helper
for a restarting peer. By default, router will participate
in the graceful restart of a neighbor with strict LSA check enabled.

instance-tag-vrf.description: Key combination of instance-tag and vrf separated by ~

max-metric.description:
sets the protocol to advertise a maximum metric so that other
routers do not prefer the router as an intermediate hop in their shortest
path first (SPF) calculations.
- `include-stub` option configures the router to advertise maximum
  metric for links including stub links in router LSA.
- `on-startup` option configures the router to advertise maximum metric in
  router LSA at startup for the time mentioned in seconds or for
  a default value of 600 seconds.
- `include-stub` and `on-startup` options together configure the router
  to advertise maximum metric for links including stub links in router LSA at
  startup for the time mentioned in seconds or for a default value of 600 seconds.

maximum-paths.description: Maximum number of equal cost paths that are stored for each destination in the Routing table.

nonstop.description: Enables or disables nonstop forwarding for OSPFv3.

passive-interface-default.description:
All OSPFv3 enabled interfaces to be passive.
An OSPFv3 passive interface is a network interface
that does not send or receive OSPFv3 Hello packets,
but still participates in the routing process for subnet advertisement.

ArubaPim_PimRouterSchemaGET.description:
Configure Protocol Independent Multicast.
In a network, IP multicast traffic transmitted for multimedia applications
is blocked at routed interface boundaries unless a multicast routing protocol
is running. Protocol Independent Multicast (PIM) is a family of routing
protocols. It forms multicast trees to forward traffic from multicast sources
to the clients requesting for traffic.

router.description: The list of all the PIM Router instances

anycast-rp-source-directly-connected.description:
Allow same multicast source to be directly connected
to two or more Anycast RP routers on IPv4 network

candidate-bsr.description:
Candidate Bootstrap Router Configuration for IPv4 network.
A candidate BSR is configured so that it is eligible to be elected as the
Bootstrap Router (BSR) in a PIM domain. This is the router that collects
information from all available RPs in the network and advertises it on the
PIM domain.

candidate-bsr-v6.description: Candidate Bootstrap Router Configuration for IPv6 network

candidate-rp.description:
Candidate RP Configuration for IPv4 network.
A candidate RP is configured to take part in the rendezvous point(RP) election in
PIM Sparse Mode(SM). To become an RP, a candidate RP will announce itself to the
bootstrap router (BSR) using unicast packets.

candidate-rp-v6.description: Candidate RP Configuration for IPv6 network

ArubaPrefixList_PrefixListsSchemaGET.description:
Prefix Lists are standard routing filters (RFC 4632) used to permit
or deny network routes based on IP address prefixes in CIDR notation.
Configure IPv4 and IPv6 prefix lists for granular route control in
BGP and OSPF routing policies. Each list contains sequential entries
with permit/deny actions and optional prefix length matching
constraints for flexible route filtering across multi-site networks.

characters. Use this API to retrieve the list of prefix list
configurations.

ArubaRip_RipSchemaGET.description:
Routing Information Protocol (RIP) is a distance-vector interior
gateway protocol (IGP) using hop count as the routing metric
with a maximum of 15 hops (RFC 2453). RIP supports RIPv1 and
RIPv2 for IPv4 networks with features including route
redistribution, authentication, route filtering, and
configurable timers. Configure RIP router instances with
administrative distance, maximum paths, timers (update,
timeout, garbage-collection), route redistribution from other
protocols, and interface-specific parameters. Use this API to
retrieve the list of RIP profiles.

ArubaRoutemap_RouteMapSet.description: Route-map Set Actions configuration. Provides comprehensive tools for manipulating routing protocol attributes and traffic policies. This container supports setting BGP Path Attributes (AS path prepending/exclusion, communities, origin, local preference, MED), OSPF metrics, tag-based traffic classification, and next-hop address overrides. Set actions enable sophisticated traffic engineering, route filtering policies, and attribute-based routing across BGP, OSPF, and other routing protocols

set-as-path.description: Modify BGP AS Path attribute. Allows prepending additional Autonomous System numbers (for increasing local preference) or excluding specific AS numbers (for path filtering). AS Path manipulation is fundamental to BGP traffic engineering and helps control route propagation patterns and neighbor selection behavior

as-exclude.description: Set AS to be excluded from AS-path.

last-as-prepend-count.description: Number of times to prepend the last AS to AS path attribute

prepend-as.description: Space-separated list of Autonomous System numbers to prepend to the route's AS path. Prepending increases the AS path length, making routes less preferred during BGP best path selection. Used for traffic engineering to influence incoming traffic direction. Each AS number in the list is prepended once in the order specified. Example: 65000 65001 (prepends AS 65000, then AS 65001)

set-community.description: BGP Community Attribute configuration. Enables setting or modifying BGP Community values to classify routes for policy application. Supports standard communities (AS:value pairs), extended communities (for VPNs), and well-known communities (INTERNET, NO_EXPORT, NO_ADVERTISE, LOCAL_AS). Community operations include additive (append) and delete (remove specific communities)

items.description: Community attribute value.

set-community-list.description: BGP community list attribute to be set

set-dampening.description: Configurations related to Routemap for Route-flap dampening

half-life.description:
Amount of time that must elapse to reduce
the penalty by one half.

max-suppress-time.description: The maximum amount of time that a route can be suppressed.

reuse-limit.description: Penalty value at which routes are made active.

suppress-limit.description:
If the penalty is greater than the suppress limit,
 the route is suppressed.

set-extended-community.description: Configurations related to setting IP extended communities.

items.description: Set route-target extended community.

router-mac.description: Set the Router MAC address extended community.
router-mac.x-typeDescription:
The mac-address type represents an IEEE 802 MAC address.
The canonical representation uses lowercase characters.

In the value set and its semantics, this type is equivalent
to the MacAddress textual convention of the SMIv2.

set-ipv4-nexthop-address.description: IPv4 next-hop address to be set for BGP routes. Overrides the default next-hop address used for routes matched by this route-map entry. Useful for policy-based route manipulation and traffic engineering to force traffic through specific next-hops or gateways. Cannot be combined with IPv6 configuration
set-ipv4-nexthop-address.x-typeDescription:
An IPv4 address in dotted quad notation using the default
zone.

set-ipv6-nexthop.description: IPv6 global next-hop address to be set for BGP routes. Overrides the default next-hop address used for IPv6 routes matched by this route-map entry. Used for policy-based route manipulation, traffic engineering, and IPv6 path selection. Cannot be combined with IPv4 configuration
set-ipv6-nexthop.x-typeDescription:
An IPv6 address represented as either a full address; shortened
or mixed-shortened formats, using the default zone.

set-local-broadcast-group.description: Set the local broadcast group id.

set-local-preference.description: BGP Local Preference attribute value to be set. Local Preference influences route selection within the local Autonomous System (all iBGP neighbors see the same value). Higher values indicate more preferred routes; routes to the same destination are compared and the route with highest local preference is selected. Default value is 100. Used for traffic engineering within your AS

set-metric.description: BGP Multi-Exit Discriminator (MED) attribute value to be set (also called metric). MED is used to influence route selection between external BGP (eBGP) neighbors in a specific AS. Lower MED values are more preferred. Unlike Local Preference, MED is advertised to external peers. Used for traffic engineering to external neighbors to indicate preferred entry points

set-origin.description: BGP Origin attribute value to be set. The Origin attribute indicates how the route was learned: IGP (Interior Gateway Protocol - learned from internal protocol like OSPF), EGP (Exterior Gateway Protocol - rare legacy usage), or Incomplete (origin unknown - common for redistributed or statically configured routes). Used in BGP best path selection as a tie-breaker

set-tag.description: Route Tag attribute. A 32-bit numeric tag value assigned to routes for classification, filtering, and tracking across routing domains. Tags are used in OSPF, static routing, and policy-based routing for route discrimination and policy application. Tags enable route identification without protocol-specific attributes

set-weight.description: BGP Weight attribute value to be set. Routes with higher weight values are preferred during BGP best path selection (weight comparison occurs before AS path length comparison). Weight has local AS significance only and is not advertised to neighbors. Default weight is 32768

ArubaRoutemap_RouteMapsSchemaGET.description:
Route Maps provide advanced routing policy control by
manipulating route attributes during redistribution between
routing protocols (BGP, OSPF, RIP, static routes). Route
maps use match conditions (IP prefix lists, community lists,
AS path lists) and set actions (metric modification,
next-hop changes, AS path prepending) to filter and transform
routing information. Configure route map sequences with
permit/deny actions to implement complex routing policies for
traffic engineering and route aggregation. Requires
prefix-list, community-list, and AS path-list configurations
for match conditions. Device Limits: Gateway restricts route
map names to 1-50 characters. Use this API to retrieve the
list of route map configurations.

route-map.description:
Route map defining policy-based route manipulation with
ordered sequences of match conditions and set actions.
Each entity is identified by route map name. Configure
permit/deny entries with match criteria (prefix-lists,
communities, AS paths) and set actions (metric, next-hop,
local-preference) for routing policy control.

Static routing is a type of network routing technique. Static routing is
not a routing protocol; instead, it is the manual configuration and
selection of a network route, usually managed by the network administrator.
It is employed in scenarios where the network routing parameters are not
expected to change often.

ArubaVrf_VrfsSchemaGET.description: Virtual Routing Forwarding (VRF) container. A VRF is a logical partition of a network device's routing infrastructure that enables multiple independent routing tables on a single device. This container manages VRF instances, each with isolated routing domains, independent routing policies, route targets, and address families (IPv4, IPv6). VRFs support multi-tenancy, network segregation, and route leaking capabilities (IVRL - Inter-VRF Route Leaking) for complex network architectures

vrf.description: List of named VRF instances. Each VRF maintains independent routing tables and policy enforcement domains. The 'default' VRF represents the global routing table. Each VRF can be associated with specific route targets for inter-VRF route leaking, enabling controlled route distribution across VRF boundaries for overlay and VPN deployments

get.description:
Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) is the standard exterior gateway
protocol (RFC 4271) for exchanging routing information between
autonomous systems on the Internet and enterprise networks. BGP
supports IPv4/IPv6 unicast, EVPN (Ethernet VPN), and multicast
address families with advanced features including route reflection,
confederation, graceful restart, and multi-path load balancing.
Configure BGP global parameters, neighbor relationships, route
redistribution, and address family-specific policies. Requires route
map, prefix list, keychain, and community list configurations for
routing policy enforcement. Use this API to retrieve BGP
configuration.

get.description:
OSPFv2 (Open Shortest Path First version 2) is a routing protocol which is
described in RFC2328 entitled OSPF Version 2. It is a Link State-based
IGP(Interior Gateway Protocol) routing protocol. It is widely used in medium
to large-sized enterprise networks.

The characteristics of OSPFv2 are:

- Provides a loop free topology using SPF algorithm.
- Allows hierarchical routing using area 0 (backbone area) as the top of the
 hierarchy.
- Supports load balancing with equal cost routes for same destination.
- OSPFv2 is a classless protocol and allows for a hierarchical design with
 VLSM(Variable length subnet masking) and route summarization.
- Provides authentication of routing messages.
- Scales enterprise size network easily with area concept.
- Provides fast convergence with triggered, incremental updates via
 Link State Advertisements (LSAs).

get.description:
OSPFv3 (Open Shortest Path First version 3) is a routing protocol which is
described in RFC5340 entitled OSPF for IPv6. It is a Link State-based IGP
(Interior Gateway Protocol) routing protocol. It is widely used with medium to
large-sized enterprise networks.

The characteristics of OSPFv3 are:

- Provides a loop free topology using SPF algorithm.
- Allows hierarchical routing using area 0 (backbone area) as the top of the
  hierarchy.
- Supports load balancing with equal cost routes for same destination.
- OSPFv3 is a classless protocol and allows for a hierarchical design with
  VLSM (Variable Length Subnet Masking) and route summarization.
- Scales enterprise size network easily with area concept.
- Provides fast convergence with triggered, incremental updates via
  Link State Advertisements (LSAs).

is blocked at routed interface boundaries unless a multicast routing protocol
is running. Protocol Independent Multicast (PIM) is a family of routing
protocols. It forms multicast trees to forward traffic from multicast sources
to the clients requesting for traffic.

get.description:
Prefix Lists are standard routing filters (RFC 4632) used to permit
or deny network routes based on IP address prefixes in CIDR notation.
Configure IPv4 and IPv6 prefix lists for granular route control in
BGP and OSPF routing policies. Each list contains sequential entries
with permit/deny actions and optional prefix length matching
constraints for flexible route filtering across multi-site networks.
Device Limits: Gateway restricts prefix list names to 1-50
characters. Use this API to retrieve the list of prefix list
configurations.

delete.description:
IPv4 or IPv6 prefix list for route filtering in routing
protocols. Each entity is identified by prefix-list-name
and address-family combination. Configure ordered
permit/deny entries with IP prefix matching and optional
prefix length constraints (ge/le) for granular route
control.

parameters.description: Composite key combining prefix-list-name and address-family separated by tilde (~). Uniquely identifies each prefix list configuration within the system. Format: prefix-list-name~address-family

get.description:
Routing Information Protocol (RIP) is a distance-vector interior
gateway protocol (IGP) using hop count as the routing metric
with a maximum of 15 hops (RFC 2453). RIP supports RIPv1 and
RIPv2 for IPv4 networks with features including route
redistribution, authentication, route filtering, and
configurable timers. Configure RIP router instances with
administrative distance, maximum paths, timers (update,
timeout, garbage-collection), route redistribution from other
protocols, and interface-specific parameters. Use this API to
retrieve the list of RIP profiles.

get.description:
Route Maps provide advanced routing policy control by
manipulating route attributes during redistribution between
routing protocols (BGP, OSPF, RIP, static routes). Route
maps use match conditions (IP prefix lists, community lists,
AS path lists) and set actions (metric modification,
next-hop changes, AS path prepending) to filter and transform
routing information. Configure route map sequences with
permit/deny actions to implement complex routing policies for
traffic engineering and route aggregation. Requires
prefix-list, community-list, and AS path-list configurations
for match conditions. Device Limits: Gateway restricts route
map names to 1-50 characters. Use this API to retrieve the
list of route map configurations.

delete.description:

ordered sequences of match conditions and set actions.
Each entity is identified by route map name. Configure
permit/deny entries with match criteria (prefix-lists,
communities, AS paths) and set actions (metric, next-hop,
local-preference) for routing policy control.

get.description:
Static routing is a type of network routing technique. Static routing is
not a routing protocol; instead, it is the manual configuration and
selection of a network route, usually managed by the network administrator.
It is employed in scenarios where the network routing parameters are not
expected to change often.
Static route profiles.

get.description: Virtual Routing Forwarding (VRF) container. A VRF is a logical partition of a network device's routing infrastructure that enables multiple independent routing tables on a single device. This container manages VRF instances, each with isolated routing domains, independent routing policies, route targets, and address families (IPv4, IPv6). VRFs support multi-tenancy, network segregation, and route leaking capabilities (IVRL - Inter-VRF Route Leaking) for complex network architectures

delete.description: List of named VRF instances. Each VRF maintains independent routing tables and policy enforcement domains. The 'default' VRF represents the global routing table. Each VRF can be associated with specific route targets for inter-VRF route leaking, enabling controlled route distribution across VRF boundaries for overlay and VPN deployments
